---
title: "Ch 2 — Relations and Functions"
subtitle: "Pairs of inputs and outputs — the spine of every formula you will ever meet"
lesson-id: "cbse11.relations-and-functions"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 2
ncert-chapter-title: "Relations and Functions"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse11.sets", "t2.functions-as-machines"]
unlocks: ["cbse11.trig-functions", "cbse11.limits", "cbse12.relations-functions-types"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "databases", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you write down a set in roster form without hesitating?
2. Do you know what an **ordered pair** $(a, b)$ is, and why $(1,2) \ne (2,1)$?
3. Can you graph $y = 2x + 1$ on paper from memory?
4. Have you ever said "f of x" out loud and meant it?

If three of these were a confident yes, read on. If sets feel shaky,
revisit **[Sets](../01-sets/index.qmd)** first.
:::

## Touch

Hold up your left hand. Hold up your right hand. Now point your left
index finger at one of your right-hand fingers. You have just made an
**ordered pair** — (left index, right ring, say). The order matters: the
left finger is doing the pointing, the right finger is being pointed at.

A **relation** is a rule that decides which pointings are allowed.

The widget below shows two small sets $A$ and $B$. Toggle the rule and
watch which arrows light up. Each arrow is one ordered pair.

```{ojs}
viewof rule = Inputs.select(["a < b", "a + b is even", "a divides b", "a equals b"], {label: "Relation rule"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const A = [1, 2, 3, 4];
  const B = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];
  const test = {
    "a < b": (a, b) => a < b,
    "a + b is even": (a, b) => (a + b) % 2 === 0,
    "a divides b": (a, b) => b % a === 0,
    "a equals b": (a, b) => a === b
  }[rule];
  const pairs = [];
  for (const a of A) for (const b of B) if (test(a, b)) pairs.push(`(${a},${b})`);
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.6;">
    <div><b>A =</b> {1,2,3,4}, <b>B =</b> {1,2,3,4,5,6}</div>
    <div><b>Relation R = {(a,b) :</b> ${rule} <b>} =</b></div>
    <div style="margin-left:1em;">{${pairs.join(", ")}}</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.5em;color:#666;">
      ${pairs.length} ordered pairs out of ${A.length * B.length} possible.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Try every rule. Every rule is a relation. A **function** will be a
particularly nice kind of relation — *every input has exactly one
output*.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. If $A$ has $3$ elements and $B$ has $4$, how many ordered pairs are
   there in $A \times B$? How many *relations* from $A$ to $B$?
2. Look at the rule "$a$ divides $b$". From the input $a = 2$, how many
   different outputs are allowed? Is this a function?
3. Two students draw graphs of "$y$ is the square root of $x$". One
   draws only the upper half of a parabola lying on its side; the other
   draws both halves. Which (if either) is correct, and why?
4. Could a graph that fails the *vertical line test* still be a useful
   relation?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

(2) gives the diagnostic for "function": a single input that maps to
two outputs is not a function. (3) is the reason $\sqrt{x}$ has to be
defined as the *non-negative* root — to keep it a function.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Cartesian product

Let $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{x, y, z\}$. Write out $A \times B$ and
$B \times A$. Are they equal?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$A \times B$ contains every pair $(a, b)$ with $a \in A$, $b \in B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A \times B = \{(1,x),(1,y),(1,z),(2,x),(2,y),(2,z)\}$ — six pairs.

$B \times A = \{(x,1),(x,2),(y,1),(y,2),(z,1),(z,2)\}$ — six pairs.

They have the same *number* of elements but are **not equal**:
$(1,x) \ne (x,1)$. Order matters in the pair, so the product depends on
which set is "first".
</details>

### Problem 2 — Domain and range of a relation

Let $R = \{(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6), (1, 7)\}$ be a relation from $A$ to
$B$. What is the domain of $R$? What is the range?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Domain = the set of all *first* coordinates that appear. Range = the
set of all *second* coordinates that appear.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Domain $= \{1, 2, 3\}$ (note: $1$ appears twice, listed once in the
domain).

Range $= \{4, 5, 6, 7\}$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Function vs relation

Which of these are functions $f : \{1,2,3\} \to \{a,b,c\}$?

(i) $\{(1,a),(2,b),(3,c)\}$
(ii) $\{(1,a),(2,a),(3,a)\}$
(iii) $\{(1,a),(1,b),(2,c),(3,a)\}$
(iv) $\{(1,a),(2,b)\}$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A function from $A$ to $B$ assigns *exactly one* element of $B$ to each
element of $A$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- (i) Function. Every input mapped, each to one output.
- (ii) Function. All inputs map to the same output — that's allowed.
- (iii) **Not a function**: input $1$ maps to two outputs.
- (iv) **Not a function**: input $3$ has no output.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Linear function

Let $f(x) = 2x - 3$. Compute $f(0)$, $f(5)$, $f(-2)$. For what $x$ is
$f(x) = 7$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For the last part, set $2x - 3 = 7$ and solve.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(0) = -3$, $f(5) = 7$, $f(-2) = -7$.

$2x - 3 = 7 \Rightarrow x = 5$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Domain from a formula

What is the largest subset of $\mathbb{R}$ on which $f(x) = \sqrt{x - 2}$
makes sense?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The square root requires its argument to be non-negative.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Need $x - 2 \ge 0$, i.e. $x \ge 2$. Domain $= [2, \infty)$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Range of a quadratic

What is the range of $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \in \mathbb{R}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Squares are never negative.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 \ge 0$ always, and every non-negative real $y$ equals $(\sqrt{y})^2$.
So range $= [0, \infty)$.
</details>

You have just used **ordered pairs**, **Cartesian products**,
**relations**, **domain**, **range**, **functions**, and the **vertical
line test** (implicitly, in Problem 3). Now we name them.

::: {.fun-fact}
For two centuries, "function" meant *a formula that you could write
down with the symbols then in fashion*. In 1837, **Peter Gustav
Lejeune Dirichlet** broke that habit. To prove a result on Fourier
series he needed a function that took the value $1$ on every rational
number and $0$ on every irrational — no formula, no graph anyone could
draw, just a *rule that decided one output for each input*. With that
example he stated the modern definition: **a function is any
correspondence assigning to each input exactly one output**. The
formula-free definition you use in this lesson is Dirichlet's, not
Newton's or Euler's.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A function is a **promise**: feed in any input from the domain,
receive *exactly one* output. Two outputs from one input breaks the
promise — that is the only thing the vertical-line test is checking.
Every other word in this chapter (relation, domain, range, codomain)
is bookkeeping around that single promise.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **Cartesian product** of sets $A$ and $B$ is
$$A \times B = \{(a, b) : a \in A,\ b \in B\}.$$

A **relation** $R$ from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$.

A **function** $f : A \to B$ is a relation in which **every** $a \in A$
appears as the first coordinate of **exactly one** pair. We write
$f(a) = b$ for the unique $b$ paired with $a$.

- The **domain** is $A$ (all permitted inputs).
- The **codomain** is $B$ (the set the outputs live in).
- The **range** is $\{f(a) : a \in A\} \subseteq B$ (the outputs actually
  hit).

**Standard real functions** (graphs you must know):

| $f(x)$ | Domain | Range |
|:--|:--|:--|
| $x$ (identity) | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $|x|$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| $x^2$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| $\sqrt{x}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| $1/x$ | $\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}$ | $\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}$ |
| $[x]$ (greatest integer) | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{Z}$ |

:::

A short verse:

> *Every input — exactly one output;*
> *No input loose, no input split.*
> *Where the formula refuses to land,*
> *That point lies outside the function's gift.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the domain and range of $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{4 - x^2}}$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Two restrictions: the radicand must be $\ge 0$, and the denominator
   must not be $0$. Combined: $4 - x^2 > 0$.
2. $4 - x^2 > 0 \Leftrightarrow x^2 < 4 \Leftrightarrow -2 < x < 2$.
3. **Domain** $= (-2, 2)$.
4. As $x$ ranges over $(-2, 2)$, $4 - x^2$ ranges over $(0, 4]$. So
   $\sqrt{4-x^2}$ ranges over $(0, 2]$, and $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{4-x^2}}$
   ranges over $[1/2, \infty)$.
5. **Range** $= [1/2, \infty)$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** If $f(x) = x^2 + 1$, compute $f(-1)$, $f(0)$, $f(3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(-1) = 2$, $f(0) = 1$, $f(3) = 10$.
</details>

**W2.** Find domain of $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x - 5}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x \ne 5$. Domain $= \mathbb{R} \setminus \{5\}$.
</details>

**W3.** If $A = \{a, b\}$ and $B = \{1, 2, 3\}$, how many *relations* are
there from $A$ to $B$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$A \times B$ has $|A| \cdot |B|$ pairs. A relation is *any* subset.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|A \times B| = 6$. Number of subsets = $2^6 = 64$ relations.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Let $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1}$. What is the largest domain?
Simplify $f$ on this domain.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Denominator zero is forbidden. Then factor the numerator.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Need $x \ne 1$. Then $f(x) = \dfrac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x-1} = x + 1$.

Domain $= \mathbb{R} \setminus \{1\}$. The graph is the line $y = x+1$
with a hole at $(1, 2)$.
</details>

**P2.** Find the range of $f(x) = |x - 3|$ for $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|y| \ge 0$ always.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|x - 3| \ge 0$ for all $x$, with equality at $x = 3$. Range $= [0, \infty)$.
</details>

**P3.** A function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is given by
$f(x) = 3x + 5$. Find $x$ such that $f(x) = 2x + 11$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set the two expressions equal and solve.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x + 5 = 2x + 11 \Rightarrow x = 6$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Let $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{1 + x^2}$. Show that the range is
$\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set $y = \dfrac{x}{1+x^2}$ and solve for $x$ in terms of $y$. The
condition for real $x$ gives the range.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Cross-multiplying: $yx^2 - x + y = 0$. For real $x$, the discriminant
must be $\ge 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y(1+x^2) = x \Rightarrow yx^2 - x + y = 0$.

If $y = 0$, then $x = 0$ — fine. If $y \ne 0$, this is a quadratic in
$x$ with discriminant $1 - 4y^2 \ge 0$, giving $y^2 \le 1/4$, i.e.
$-\tfrac{1}{2} \le y \le \tfrac{1}{2}$.

Both endpoints are attained at $x = \pm 1$. Range $= [-\tfrac{1}{2},
\tfrac{1}{2}]$.
</details>

**S2.** Find $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x) = ax + b$ satisfies
$f(1) = 4$ and $f(3) = 10$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two equations in two unknowns.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a + b = 4$, $3a + b = 10$. Subtract: $2a = 6$, so $a = 3$, $b = 1$.

$f(x) = 3x + 1$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** If $f(x) = 2x + 1$ and $f(a) = 7$, then $a$ is
(a) $2$ (b) $3$ (c) $4$ (d) $7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2a + 1 = 7 \Rightarrow a = 3$. **Answer: (b).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the domain of
$f(x) = \sqrt{9 - x^2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$9 - x^2 \ge 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 \le 9 \Leftrightarrow -3 \le x \le 3$. Domain $= [-3, 3]$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Let $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $B = \{4, 5\}$.
Write $A \times B$ and the relation $R = \{(a, b) \in A \times B :
a + b \le 6\}$ in roster form.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A \times B = \{(1,4),(1,5),(2,4),(2,5),(3,4),(3,5)\}$.

$R$: pairs with sum $\le 6$:
$(1,4), (1,5), (2,4)$. So $R = \{(1,4),(1,5),(2,4)\}$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Let $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 2x + 1}{x^2 - 1}$.

(i) Find the largest domain.
(ii) Simplify $f$ on this domain.
(iii) Sketch the graph.
(iv) State the range.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor numerator and denominator. Cancel where allowed, but track the
forbidden $x$ values.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Numerator $(x+1)^2$, denominator $(x-1)(x+1)$. Need $x \ne \pm 1$.

(i) Domain $= \mathbb{R} \setminus \{-1, 1\}$.

(ii) For $x \ne -1$, $f(x) = \dfrac{(x+1)^2}{(x-1)(x+1)} = \dfrac{x+1}{x-1}$.
Still $x \ne 1$.

(iii) Graph of $y = \dfrac{x+1}{x-1} = 1 + \dfrac{2}{x-1}$: hyperbola
shifted to vertex at $(1, 1)$ with vertical asymptote $x = 1$ and
horizontal asymptote $y = 1$. There's a *hole* at $x = -1$ (value
$0$ there but excluded).

(iv) Range: solve $y = \dfrac{x+1}{x-1}$ for $x$:
$y(x-1) = x+1 \Rightarrow x(y-1) = y+1 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{y+1}{y-1}$.
This requires $y \ne 1$. Also the hole at $x = -1$ excludes $y = 0$.

Range $= \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0, 1\}$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 2 Q "Find domain and range of
real-valued function" is the direct ancestor of P2 and the 2-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** range-via-discriminant (Stretch S1) is a Main-level
classic. Practice it.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [function](/glossary.qmd#function) is a [relation](/glossary.qmd#relation) where every input has exactly one output** — no input split, no input missing.
2. **The [domain](/glossary.qmd#domain) is the set of inputs the formula will accept**; the [range](/glossary.qmd#range) is the set of outputs it actually hits — and range is usually a strict subset of the codomain.
3. **For real functions, hidden domain restrictions hide in two places**: denominators that must not vanish and even-root [radicands](/glossary.qmd#radicand) that must not go negative.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Codomain and range are the same."** They are not. Codomain is the
   *declared* output set ($B$). Range is the set of values $f$ actually
   *hits*. Range $\subseteq$ codomain, often strictly.
2. **"$\sqrt{x}$ has two values, $+$ and $-$."** Not as a function. The
   symbol $\sqrt{x}$ denotes the *non-negative* root only. Otherwise it
   would not be a function.
3. **"Every relation is a function."** No. Relations are arbitrary
   subsets of $A \times B$. Functions are the special ones where each
   input maps to exactly one output.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain why "$y^2 = x$" is not a function (of $x$) without
   notes?
2. Did you find domain and range without re-reading the table at least
   five times during practice?
3. Can you spot the hidden domain restrictions in any rational or radical
   expression?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Trigonometric Functions](../03a-trig-functions/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S1 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** redo *Discover*. Functions are the language of every
  remaining chapter — fluency now pays off all year.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Sets](../01-sets/index.qmd)** — relations and functions are subsets
  of $A \times B$.
- **[Functions as Machines](../../tier-2-prealgebra/functions-as-machines/index.qmd)** —
  the input-out-with-one-output picture, formalised here.

### What this unlocks

- **[Trigonometric Functions](../03a-trig-functions/index.qmd)** — the
  six trig functions, defined on $\mathbb{R}$.
- **[Limits](../12a-limits/index.qmd)** — limits of *functions* near a
  point.
- **[Class 12 — Relations & Functions](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/01-relations-functions-types/index.qmd)** —
  one-one, onto, bijective, inverse functions.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Sections 2.1–2.5: Cartesian product, relations, domain
  and range, functions, real functions, algebra of real functions.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t2.functions-as-machines`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/functions-as-machines/index.qmd)
  and
  [`t2.linear-functions-graphs`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)
  give an intuition-first treatment.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** A neural network is a function $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to
\mathbb{R}^m$, the composition of millions of simple functions. Every
"forward pass" is just function evaluation. Training adjusts the weights
so $f$ matches a target relation drawn from data.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** A *foreign key* in SQL is a relation between two tables.
A primary key plus a foreign key create a **functional** relation —
exactly one row in the parent table per row in the child. The integrity
constraints of a relational database are *exactly* the conditions for a
relation to be a function.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** "Function" is the central object of analysis, the source
of every named theorem from intermediate-value to spectral. Class 11's
modest definition is the seed.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (functions as transformations), Better
Explained (Aha framing: a function is a *promise* of one output per
input), Russian school (Discover sequence forces the function vs
relation distinction), Bhāskara (verse summarises the
exactly-one-output rule).
:::
